slides.title: What is Mean Squared Error (MSE)?
slides.content:
MSE is a measure of the quality of an estimator.
It calculates the average squared difference between estimated and actual values.

slides.title: Why MSE?
slides.content:
- Provides a clear differentiation between high and low error values
- Squaring amplifies larger errors

visual_assets.text: "The smaller the MSE, the closer the fit is to the data points."

slides.title: MSE Formula
slides.content:
The formula for MSE is expressed as:

MSE = (1/n) * Σ(actual - predicted)²

visual_assets.code:
def calculate_mse(actual, predicted):
    n = len(actual)
    mse = sum((a - p) ** 2 for a, p in zip(actual, predicted)) / n
    return mse
visual_assets.caption: Python function to calculate MSE

slides.title: MSE in Action
slides.content:
Consider a model predicting house prices.
Predicted: [300k, 500k, 700k]
Actual: [310k, 490k, 680k]

visual_assets.code:
predicted = [300000, 500000, 700000]
actual = [310000, 490000, 680000]
print(calculate_mse(actual, predicted))  # Output: MSE value
visual_assets.caption: Applying MSE to a prediction model

slides.title: MSE vs. Other Metrics
slides.content:
- Compared to MAE (Mean Absolute Error):
  - MSE penalizes larger errors more
  - MAE is more robust to outliers

visual_assets.text: Choosing the right error metric is crucial for evaluating model performance effectively.

slides.title: Reducing MSE
slides.content:
To reduce MSE, try:

- Using more data
- Enhancing model complexity
- Optimizing hyperparameters

visual_assets.text: "Model improvement is a continuous journey of error reduction."

slides.title: Conclusion
slides.content:
MSE is a fundamental metric for regression models.
Understanding and minimizing MSE can significantly enhance model performance.
